title: The Day I Was Sick on the American Revolution
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
I was sick on the august steps of the New York Historical Society.

Not, I regret to say, in a delicate, interesting or metaphorical fashion.

It was the bright pink result of having been persuaded to drink something called Gatorade. I had jet-lag, it was very hot in New York last August, and I’m just a terrible traveller in general.

‘Electrolytes,’ my colleagues told me. ‘You’ll feel better.’

I did not.

My shaming experience was, in its way, a fitting introduction to the American Revolution.

It’s a story that looks straightforward from a distance: rousing, inspiring and refreshing. A nasty king is defied. A new nation is born.

But it turns out, on closer inspection, to be a bit more complicated.

Our new series, Lucy Worsley Investigates the American Revolution, has just premiered on PBS, and I believe it’ll be on BBC Two later in this 250th anniversary year.

‘What’s a Brit doing on our patch?’ American viewers might reasonably ask.

The answer is, hopefully, bringing a slightly different perspective.

Because if you learned about the American Revolution at school, you were probably taught to think of it in terms of battles, tactics, and heroics on North American soil.

But what interested me was something a bit different.

What did this revolution do to Britain?

And was the whole thing inevitable? Or could someone, somewhere, have stopped it before it reached the point of no return?

Filming took us back and forth across the Atlantic.

One day we were in Britain, in the kinds of grand interiors where imperial decisions were made — including a memorable visit to the Royal Library at Windsor Castle.

The next, we were in America, where the consequences of those decisions were felt in much more immediately.

By the time we reached Yorktown — or ‘Surrender Field’, as the scene of Britain’s final defeat is rather brutally named to British ears — the heat and humidity of Virginia in August were so intense that our clothes were clinging to us before we’d even started work. It wasn’t helped by local people telling us that this was in fact a beautifully cool, fresh day and that we should count ourselves lucky.

At the end of that last, extremely damp day, the crew and I did what all sensible historians do.

We jumped into the Chesapeake.

The ‘wrap swim’ was glorious.

The two-hour drive to Richmond afterwards, in wet clothes, was less so. But we revived our spirits with a celebratory pizza close to midnight. We were still damp, dirty and smelling faintly of river water — but very happy.

It was a wonderful trip, not just because of the places, but because of the people.

One of the great pleasures of making history programmes is meeting those who care deeply about the past — curators, historians, enthusiasts — each adding another layer to the story.

What struck me most, working on both sides of the Atlantic, was how differently the same events can feel depending on where you stand.

In Britain, the Revolution often appears as a problem of top-down management: a question of government policies, tax laws and crowd control.

In America, it feels much more immediate: a story of risk and division and individuals experiencing horrible uncertainty.

The one thing it absolutely isn’t, on either side, is simple.

Do consider becoming a paid subscriber to MY LIFE IN THE PAST, for the deeper, paid-subscriber-only pieces that come out every other week!

Subscribe now

Take loyalty, for example.

We often talk as though ‘the Americans’ rose up together.

In fact, they were deeply divided. Many remained loyal to George III, some out of conviction, some because rebellion looked extraordinarily dangerous. Choosing a side could split families, end livelihoods, and force people into exile.

And then there’s the king himself.

George III has acquired a reputation as the villain of the piece. But when you return to the documents, he emerges as something much more complicated: a conscientious and very human ruler trying to hold together an increasingly unwieldy empire.

I bet lots of Americans, even Britons, don’t realise that the American Revolution also played out as arson attacks on British naval dockyards, an invasion in Jersey, government concessions in Ireland, and seven days of rioting in London.

The tragedy is that both sides believed they were acting reasonably.

The American Revolution is often presented as a beginning, and of course it is.

But it is also the end of a long chain of misunderstandings, compromises, and missed chances — on both sides of the Atlantic.

I think that makes it a much less comfortable and cheerful story that the rousing one that celebrates the birth of a new nation. But I think it makes it a more interesting one too.

What did you learn about the American Revolution at school? And have you visited one of its battlefields?

Leave a comment

title: From Hampton Court to Here: My New Book!
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
The cover is here.

And soon – all too soon, given there’s lots of work still to do – the book will be here too!

A little over a year ago, I walked out of my office at Hampton Court Palace for the final time.

I remember leaving the light on, and the door open. Not by accident. I wasn’t really ready to shut that chapter.

And I haven’t! Since then, I’ve been writing hard, and now I can finally show you what I’ve been up to.

This week, the cover has been revealed for QUEENS AND KINGS, AN UNUSUALLY PERSONAL HISTORY OF THE MONARCHY.

I’d love it if you’d consider pre-ordering, which is the single most helpful thing you can do for an author you’d like to support!

→ Pre-order here (including signed copies)

I’m particularly pleased to be working with so many independent bookshops to offer signed copies. To order an actual book through an actual bricks-and-mortar bookshop is, these days, to indulge in a tiny bit of hope and defiance.

In short, pre-ordering my book will make you feel good!

What kind of book is this?

It’s not quite like anything I’ve written before.

Usually, I disappear into archives and emerge with huge swathes of referenced sources.* This time, I’ve done something rather different. In a sense, I’ve been researching this book for the past twenty years.

It includes the stories, moments, and people from my two decades of working inside palace walls that have stayed with me. The things I’ve found most surprising, most moving, or simply too good not to tell.

And alongside them runs something else: the story of how my own understanding of the past has changed over time.

I began working at Hampton Court when I was 29, and at first I actually lived inside the palace — in Fish Court, and then in the Barrack Block. I remember how overwhelming it was. I couldn’t find my way around without help.

For the first few years, it felt like I spent all my time just asking questions.

But I was also determined that one day I might be the person answering them.

I suppose this book is, in part, the result.

The chapters are short — close in spirit to these little essays you get in your inbox each week — and I’ve written them as if I were just telling you the story directly, face to face. I haven’t included anything I felt I ‘ought’ to. Just what’s truly lingered on in my mind.

Hey - what do you think of the cover? Do you like it?

Leave a comment

What does your gentle author do next?

This week my job is to narrate the audiobook of QUEENS AND KINGS, and being shut in a small room with just my own prose for company is always a slightly peculiar experience.

But I’ve found myself enjoying it. Not because I’m admiring my text, I’m not that much of a megalomaniac, but because I’m reminded how extraordinary these stories are. Some of the tales from the bizarre lost land of royal history are stranger than fiction.

Meanwhile, in the final stages of production, I’ve been doing one of the more nerve-wracking jobs: checking the family tree.

It runs from Henry VII to Charles III, and at one point existed on a huge roll of paper about three metres long. Calling it a family tree isn’t quite right. It’s more like a national park.

My task now is to make sure every name is correct, every date is right, and, crucially, that nobody’s been accidentally missed out along the way.

I’ve also just finalised the book’s dedication.

It’s for the lovely people, the best people in the world, who made my years at the palaces as special as they were, and it’s going to read:

This book is dedicated to everyone who loves and looks after the Historic Royal Palaces, especially their past, present and future curators.

If you’d like to be among the very first people to read the book when it’s published on 3 September, you can pre-order it (did I mention that already?) right here: https://bio.to/QueensAndKings

And thank you! Your support means the world to me!

Lucy

*Are there footnotes? Yes, of course there are footnotes. I couldn’t possibly do without them.

Subscribe now

title: The Four History Questions I’m Always Asked
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
People always seem to want to know which moment in history I’d change, which historical figure I’d most like to meet, and where in the world I’d go if cost and geography were no obstacle.

So here are some of the questions I’m asked most often — and the answers I usually give.

Thank you so much, dear readers, for your support!

1. If you could turn back the clock, which single event in history would you change?*

I would go back to 28 June 1491, to the Tudor royal palace beside the Thames at Greenwich.

Inside, the atmosphere would have been intense. Elizabeth of York, the wife of Henry VII, was in labour. What I would change is very simple: the gender of the baby.

Instead of Henry VIII, imagine the child born that day had been a girl.

Henry’s real-life sister Margaret Tudor, who later became Queen of Scotland, clearly possessed something of her brother’s formidable personality and political capacity. I often wonder what might have happened if the Tudor dynasty had been ruled by Queen Margaret I of England instead of King Henry VIII.

I think it’s highly likely there’d have been fewer executions in her reign, not least because I can’t imagine that she’d have wanted to marry Anne Boleyn.

And possibly, without the intense religious disputes that began in Henry VIII’s reign but persisted throughout the century, there’d had been a little less religious bloodshed.

If Henry VIII had been born a girl, the Tudor century might have looked very different.

2. If you could meet any figure from history, who would it be?

Florence Nightingale!

Her reputation has swung dramatically over time. To the Victorian press she was the angelic heroine of the Crimean War — the gentle ‘Lady with the Lamp.’

But modern historians have uncovered a very different personality: analytical, intellectually formidable, and sometimes deeply intimidating.

Her real talent lay not in sentiment, but in statistics, logic, and administration. She used data to transform hospital conditions and public health. In later life, she went to bed and never got up again. Perhaps it was because she was really ill.

Or, I suspect, she perhaps couldn’t be bothered to deal with her adoring public - and could get the work she wanted done through writing thousands of letters to her reformer friends.

If we met, I suspect she might eat me for breakfast.

But I admire her enormously — not least because the revisionist history of her life paints her as archetypically ‘bloody difficult.’ And those are usually my favourite women of all.

3. If you could visit any historical landmark tomorrow, where would you go?

There is one place I deeply regret missing.

Years ago, I was in Russia filming a BBC Four series about the Romanov dynasty. We covered everything — from the splendour of the imperial court to its shocking end in the Revolution of 1918.

But we never visited the Treasury of the Kremlin, where the Romanov crown jewels are kept. Why not? For the very simple reason that we couldn’t afford the enormous location fees.

So I have never seen those extraordinary, glittering symbols of a vanished empire in person.

If it ever became possible to visit Russia again (which, at the moment, sadly seems unlikely) I would go at the drop of a tiara.

4. Who is your unsung history hero?

My own history teacher, Mr King.

During my A-levels we spent a great deal of time studying the Nazis — which may sound grim, but it was during those classes that I realised I wanted to become a historian.

Many years later, he came to a public talk I was giving. I had no idea he would be there, so seeing him in the audience was a wonderful surprise. I mentioned the moment on social media afterwards. Within hours what felt like hundreds of his former pupils got in touch to say that he had inspired them too.

Great teachers don’t often get recognised for the work they do, quietly changing their pupils’ lives.

But one thing never changed for me. Even after all those years, even now, I could never call him John.

He’d always be Mr King.

if you enjoyed this, let’s do it next week too!

Now I’m curious.

If you could change one moment in history — or meet one historical figure — which would you choose?

Tell me in the comments!

Leave a comment

*To be honest, I don’t really approve of this question. Twenty years ago there was a fashion for ‘counter-factual’ history, ie. imagining what would have happened if various different twists of fate had taken place. I’m in a WhatsApp group for historians which got onto this issue this week. Hats off to member Jonathan Healey who pointed out that the great E.P. Thompson described counter-factual history as ‘Geschichtswissenschlopff,’ usually translated as ‘unhistorical sh*t.’

title: My grandmother was missing from history
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
Sometimes, if I want a button, or zip, or a hook-and-eye, I ask my mum to look in her sewing box.

She often produces some ancient item that came from the Birmingham draper’s shop run, until 1968, by one Edna Kay.

As well as being a shopkeeper to her fingertips, and a resident of Birmingham through two World Wars, Edna was my grandmother.

Join us! This is the place for a friendly weekly bite of history.

To me, of course, she was just my Granny, who spoiled me, and with whom I’d watch The Val Doonican Show. But I now realise that she had a whole life I knew nothing about.

I’ve been learning about this because I’ve just recorded an episode of the Findmypast podcast called A Family History of Wartime Women. The ‘wartime woman’ in question is my grandmother, Edna Bourne (later Kay) (1911-1986).

We’ve been piecing her life together from certificates, registers, addresses and the occasional archival crumb.

There really isn’t very much information, because this was quite an ordinary life, very little like the queens and kings with whom I spend much of my time.

But her experiences, surprising dramatic at times, are no less revealing – actually, perhaps they’re more revealing - about how most Britons lived.

Edna started out by being missing from the census, which rather pleases me.

On 2 April 1911, census takers went up and down Dugdale Street in Birmingham, noting down 89 households in tidy ink: names, ages and occupations.

My great-grandfather Thomas Bourne appears at number 17 as a ‘lathe fitter’. He was, as far as we can tell, always in work and highly respectable. My great-grandmother Lucy is there too, but, like millions of married women, her occupation column is left blank.

She was presumably pretty busy, with her husband and daughter Doris to look after in this working-class neighbourhood, surrounded by the factories, where no one had too much of anything nice.

Some women are missing from the 1911 census in Birmingham because they refused to take part as a protest against not having the vote. But not Edna. She arrived ten days too late, being born on 12 April.

Her city, Birmingham, was gearing up for war. Her father’s being a ‘lathe fitter’ is significant when you remember that in Birmingham would in 1914 become the front-line of the Home Front in the First World War, producing armaments in enormous quantities. My grandmother’s earliest memories would have been of wartime Britain — queues, shortages, and anxious adult conversations, maybe about the Zeppelin raids across the Midlands that were kept out of the papers for fear of causing alarm.

Fast-forward to 1939. Edna pops up again in a register: now 28 years old, an assistant in a boot shop assistant, still living with her parents, but at a ‘better’ address, 13 Witton Lane. It’s right by the Aston Villa football ground, the team which our family still supports.

Birmingham in 1939 was again bracing itself for war, but Edna doesn’t seem to have been called up for any war work. Customers still got their boots.

In October 1941, though, she married Henry (Harry) Kay at the register office. The certificate is neat and clear, with signatures in ink. But life is not.

Edna in fact was heavily pregnant. You can count the months from the birth certificate. Wartime Britain was complicated, and so were relationships.

And then there was the shocking fact we only discovered in 2020. I’m not going to tell you what it was – you’ll have to listen to the podcast! – but suffice it to say that our family is delightfully larger than we thought it was.

In January 1942, Edna gave birth, but six days later, the baby — my uncle, Paul — died.

His cause of death was ‘inanition and prematurity.’ It’s certainly possible that the shortages of the Blitz period just past and the disruptions to healthcare explain his failure to thrive. Edna never spoke of him, and that silence tells its own story. This wasn’t a generation who had much time for grief.

But then in 1945 my mother Enid was born, and from this point I know a lot more about Edna’s life, now running her own shop, because my mother remembers it.

Until, that is, the draper’s shop was pulled down to build Birmingham’s notorious traffic interchange, Spaghetti Junction. Sadly, my grandmother then stopped being a business owner, and started to work in other people’s shops.

This isn’t the kind of life that featured speeches, or medals, or which appears in textbooks.

But even so I’m glad as well as being able to remember my granny, I’ve now got to know Edna Bourne (1911-1986). She lived quite a little quiet life, but she lived it long enough to give me my mum, and that’s big enough for me.

Thank you for subscribing! Your support makes a difference!

What about you - do you know as much as you’d like to about your own grandmother?

Leave a comment

Some women are missing from the 1911 census in Birmingham because they refused to take part as a protest against not having the vote. But not Edna. She arrived ten days too late, being born on 12 April. Her city, Birmingham, was gearing up for war. Her father’s being a ‘lathe fitter’ is significant when you remember that in Birmingham would in 1914 become the front-line of the Home Front in the First World War, producing armaments in enormous quantities. My grandmother’s earliest memories would have been of wartime Britain — queues, shortages, and anxious adult conversations, maybe about the Zeppelin raids across the Midlands that were kept out of the papers for fear of causing alarm. Fast-forward to 1939. Edna pops up again in a register: now 28 years old, an assistant in a boot shop assistant, still living with her parents, but at a ‘better’ address, 13 Witton Lane. It’s right by the Aston Villa football ground, the team which our family still supports. Birmingham in 1939 was again bracing itself for war, but Edna doesn’t seem to have been called up for any war work. Customers still got their boots. In October 1941, though, she married Henry (Harry) Kay at the register office. The certificate is neat and clear, with signatures in ink. But life is not. Edna in fact was heavily pregnant. You can count the months from the birth certificate. Wartime Britain was complicated, and so were relationships. And then there was the shocking fact we only discovered in 2020. I’m not going to tell you what it was – you’ll have to listen to the podcast! – but suffice it to say that our family is delightfully larger than we thought it was. In January 1942, Edna gave birth, but six days later, the baby — my uncle, Paul — died. His cause of death was ‘inanition and prematurity.’ It’s certainly possible that the shortages of the Blitz period just past and the disruptions to healthcare explain his failure to thrive. Edna never spoke of him, and that silence tells its own story. This wasn’t a generation who had much time for grief. But then in 1945 my mother Enid was born, and from this point I know a lot more about Edna’s life, now running her own shop, because my mother remembers it. Until, that is, the draper’s shop was pulled down to build Birmingham’s notorious traffic interchange, Spaghetti Junction. Sadly, my grandmother then stopped being a business owner, and started to work in other people’s shops. This isn’t the kind of life that featured speeches, or medals, or which appears in textbooks. But even so I’m glad as well as being able to remember my granny, I’ve now got to know Edna Bourne (1911-1986). She lived quite a little quiet life, but she lived it long enough to give me my mum, and that’s big enough for me. Thank you for subscribing! Your support makes a difference! What about you - do you know as much as you’d like to about your own grandmother? Leave a comment

title: Lucy Worsley and Anthony Delaney on the hidden history of lovers and lawbreakers: QUEER GEORGIANS
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
Thank you to the ever-so-many of you who tuned in last night to hear talking about queer history in the eighteenth century!

It was wonderful to have lots of newcomers to MY LIFE IN THE PAST who are fans of Anthony’s HUGE history podcast, After Dark.

I said I’d share the two books Anthony recommended, which are:

Alan Bray. Homosexuality in Renaissance England. London: Gay Men’s Press, 1982.

Jen Manion. Female Husbands: A Trans History. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2020.

But of course don’t miss Anthony’s own latest: published as Queer Georgians, A Hidden History of Lovers, Lawbreakers and Homemakers in the UK, and in the US as Queer Enlightenments.

I’m currently working on another super-exciting guest for next time - can’t wait to see you then!

Lucy

title: TO MY DEAR READERS
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
I have always believed that the past is not dead. It is not even past. I think it lives on all around us in the world today.

One year on from my starting MY LIFE IN THE PAST, I thought I’d share what I’ve learned along the way with you, my dear readers, old and new.

I believe – and I think you might believe too – that history doesn’t just lie in dates and the deeds of great men.

Subscribe now

I think it can also be traced in the way we look after our bodies, the way we use our rooms, the rituals we cling to, and in the funny little objects that linger on in the world long after they’ve stopped being useful.

I like to try to re-tell old stories to bring out new details, from the viewpoint of people who didn’t control the narrative at the time.

MY LIFE IN THE PAST is where I enjoy exploring all this.

I really enjoy writing to you every week. It’s a bit different to packing lots of information into a 30 second piece to camera, or into a sixty-minute lecture with a set of Powerpoint slides. And it’s a very welcome break from slogging away at the 110,000-word history book I’ve just handed in!

When I pop up in your inbox, it’s purely as a nosy historian enjoying herself, trying to understand how the people before us once lived - and also what that might tell us about ourselves.

What I want to do here is to answer questions such as:

  • What did it feel like to live the lives of celebrated women like Jane Austen and Agatha Christie?

  • Why do we care so much about the fate of Notre Dame Cathedral?

  • What kinds of riddles might be hidden in wallpaintings?

  • What happens if we look at the past through the eyes of women rather than men?

What You’ll Find Here

Subscribers to MY LIFE IN THE PAST receive essays about topics that would never get made into an hour of television. They are quirkier, more personal and occasionally more mischievous.

You’ll find:

Domestic history — I love learning about the nitty gritty dirty detail of daily life, and I think that sometimes knowing how people washed their socks or treated their illnesses can open up a window into the lost world of the past.

Royal history — but from the left-field. I like taking apart the old narratives, and trying to find fresher, more relevant ways of telling our national stories.

Material history - the objects that made up daily life: beds, kettles, calling cards, gloves. I’ll always be a curator at heart, someone who loves things.

The history of one historian - I’ve also enjoyed sharing with you the silly, humbling, ridiculous and joyous side of my life and work.

I think what really gets me out of bed in the mornings is wanting to learn about the friction that can exist between past and present.

I love the moment when I’m researching something from the past and I realise that we’re not living in a better way than them - just differently.

Why Subscribe?

Of course, there’s loads and loads of brilliant free history online.

But what paid subscribers value here is something a bit different. They’ve told me they like:

-the way these little essays aren’t rushed. They’re about things I care about, and some of them I’ve thought deeply about for years.

-the way we go down all sorts of strange corridors. Here, I don’t have to worry about sticking to the broad highway that most TV programmes travel, because I’ve come to feel confident that readers enjoy a detour too!

-not taking things too seriously. I believe that history can give pleasure without being trivial, and I take the business of making history as fun as possible extremely seriously.

-the community. The comments section here is unusually delightful. People share thoughts on topic, off topic, and sometimes quite personal insights about their lives. I love logging into what feels like my own little salon.

And perhaps most of all, readers seem to like having permission to care about small things.

In traditional history, grand narratives dominate. But here we can examine the history of the fork, of clothes, of crime, of the relics of dead queens, of arcane conspiracies involving princes, of minor figures in the background of the past, like Lady Anne Clifford, or historical novelist Jean Plaidy.

Here, the little bits and bobs of the past matter, and the little people of the past, mattered too.

Subscribe now

That’s what I believe, and if you believe that too, then MY LIFE IN THE PAST is the right place for you!

Welcome!

Lucy x

Now I’m planning the year ahead on MY LIFE IN THE PAST, what would you like to read more of (or less of!) in editions to come?

Leave a comment

title: Working-class lives in Victorian London with Jacqueline Riding, author of HARD STREETS
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
Thank you, dear friends, for tuning into my live video with this evening!

We talked about Charlie Chaplin, the hard lives of his music hall performer parents, life in the workhouse and asylum, middle-class reform movements, how the arts lifted some working-class Londoners out of poverty, Doulton’s world-famous pottery, a celebrated local Victorian murder, the all-female South London gang The Forty Thieves, and why south London is better than north London!

Learn more about Jacqui’s wonderful book Hard Streets: Working Class Lives in Charlie Chaplin’s London here.

And see you soon I hope!

title: Two Sarahs catch a killer
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
One particular scene in VICTORIAN MURDER CLUB, a TV series about the Thames Torso Murderer who killed four women in London between 1887 and 1889, has inspired quite a few keyboard warriors to get in touch.

It’s the bit where I ask a pathologist if the Victorian police surgeons did a good job on the original autopsies of the victims, and she says ‘yes’.

‘Gotcha!’ the emails say. ‘You were obviously hoping the police had messed up but they hadn’t. More fool you.’

Join us! We’re friendly!

What my clever gentlemen correspondents don’t realise is that the documentary was framed as an investigation because … it was one.

I didn’t know what we were going to find. We had to explore all the dead ends.

But still, these emails got me thinking … did the Victorian police do a good job?

Well, they certainly tried hard! But they didn’t identify the murderer.

I think that now, today, we have a pretty good idea who he was.

But the mystery was solved using a couple of techniques that just weren’t available to the Victorian police.

First, though, let me fill you in on the background.

In October, 1889, a woman called Sarah Warburton accepted a lift home at night in a rowing skiff on the Thames. The boat was operated by a waterman, the Victorian equivalent of a taxi driver.

39 years old, Sarah was separated from her husband. A mother of three, she was illiterate, and earned a meagre living sewing fur trimmings onto clothes.

She’d been in a pub south of the river, but her home was on the north bank. The new Tower Bridge was still only half finished, hence the need for the boat ride. As she got into this friendly waterman’s skiff, another woman reassured Sarah that ‘she would be perfectly safe with him.’

She was wrong. He went on to rape her on the river.

But on top of that, I believe Sarah Warburton’s attacker was also the Victorian serial killer known as the Thames Torso Murderer.

He’s called this because he dismembered his victims, leaving body parts in and around the river. By the time Sarah Warburton got into his boat in October 1889, four headless torsos had been found: at Rainham, Essex (1887), Whitehall (1888), Battersea (June 1889) and Whitechapel (September 1889).

The murders remained unsolved for 130 years.

But it’s now time to showcase the work of another Sarah: a modern researcher called Sarah Bax Horton, who has – to my satisfaction at least – discovered the culprit.

A former Foreign Office analyst who’s made an inspiring mid-life jump into a new career as a historian, Sarah Bax Horton’s published her work in her book Arm of Eve.

The evidence wouldn’t stand up in a court of law, but it’s still compelling.

Sarah Bax Horton’s breakthrough was to use something the Victorian police could not: a digital newspaper database. She searched it using three filters: the timeframe of the murders, the river itself, and for violent crimes against women.

Building up what today’s criminologists - with knowledge the Victorian police didn’t have - would call a profile of the killer, Sarah Bax Horton had deduced that the Torso Murderer probably committed a string of lesser crimes as well.

Out of Sarah’s search popped the rape of Sarah Warburton, and the name of the waterman who assaulted her: James Crick.

James Crick’s movements perfectly fit the timeline of the crimes, and he had something crucial for these river-based attacks: the privacy and mobility of his boat. And there’s more: the newspapers further reveal that after earlier accusations of domestic violence and rape, the police had failed to convict Crick.

If they had done this, his later victims might have lived.

During his attack on Sarah Warburton, Crick told her he would ‘settle’ her, ‘as I have done other women that have been found in the Thames.’

But this time, a passing police officer heard Sarah’s screams and arrested him.

It was Sarah Warburton’s courtroom testimony, bravely given, that put him away in prison.

And after that, the Thames Torso Murders stopped.

Now the series is finished, I’m raising a glass to the two women who succeeded where the Victorian police failed.

I love the fact they’re both named Sarah.

Firstly, the nineteenth-century Sarah Warburton, whose testimony put the Thames Torso Murderer behind bars.

And the twenty-first century Sarah Bax Horton, whose patient detective work allows us to know and celebrate that fact.

My Life in the Past is supported by history-lovers like YOU! Join us?

For your diary: on Sunday 8 February, 7pm London time, subscribers will be enjoying our first live video chat of 2026!

I’ll be talking to historian and good friend Jacqueline Riding about her new book Hard Streets. It’s about Charlie Chaplin’s family and early life, and the history of working class lives in general in the part of South London where we both live. Join us!

title: Eight marvellous history destinations I visited in 2025
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
For 21 years my commute took me to Hampton Court Palace.

Sounds like a pretty good routine, huh?

But regular readers will know that even though I had my dream job there, I wanted to see more of the world. So, at the start of the year I packed up my office and left, with the aim of travelling more.

Where did 2025 take me?

Today’s the LAST DAY to get a half-price annual subscription on MY LIFE IN THE PAST!

  1. The Doge’s Palace, Venice, Italy

Anyone else think they would have enjoyed ten days in Venice? What a golden October I had in the city, working in the early mornings and late nights to bring you a new programme from The Doge’s Palace. I loved learning about the beautiful palace’s Black Legend of surveillance, control and torture.

  1. Surrender Field, Yorktown, Virginia

Look out in 2026 for a new series on the American Revolution! In August I was in Virginia at the battlefield where General Cornwallis and his men finally gave in to the Americans. Or, as you’ll see in the show, to the Americans AND THE FRENCH. Well-informed readers will know that beating the Brits required a bit of help from friends.

3. Sandringham, Norfolk, Great Britain

A regular and beloved part of my annual routine is taking groups to the king’s country home. Along with Balmoral, this is one of only two houses built and occupied continuously by the royal family. As well as being a time capsule version of a High Victorian estate, I think it also shows the deep eccentricity of the Windsors off duty. And, if you like, you can come with me next year!

4. Guernsey, The Channel Islands

Did you know that part of the British Isles was occupied by Nazi Germany?

Yes, it was Guernsey, and it’s a place where the Second World War seems particularly close, through the many surviving gun emplacements, batteries, and underground tunnels. I was there for happier reasons: to celebrate the 80th anniversary of its liberation.

5. Neuschwanstein Castle, Bavaria.

Known to the world as the inspiration for Walt Disney’s castle, visiting Neuschwanstein was a dream come true. Built by the sad, mad King Ludwig II of Bavaria, I loved my week of working here at nights after the visitors had left, for a programme you’ll get to see in 2026. (Even if we ate nothing for a week but cheese sandwiches!)

6. Stonor Park, Oxfordshire.

I’ve never been inside this stately home in the Chilterns, which featured in the James Bond film The Living Daylights. (Unpopular opinion alert: Timothy Dalton is ‘my’ James Bond, because when I was fourteen I went on a Girl Guides trip to London, and we were taken to The Living Daylights in Leicester Square.) But I was there this year to run a cross country race!

7. Notre Dame Cathedral, Paris, France

Having visited several times during the rebuilding project, this was the wonderful year I got to see the interior finished. It was rich, colourful, rather too full of people, but nevertheless completely wonderful.

8. Mantua, Italy

I’d always wanted to visit the Renaissance city of Mantua, and I’m so grateful my Italian publishers made it happen. I went to talk about my biography of Agatha Christie, and I do marvel at the way the Queen of Crime has taken me around the world. Another thing for which to thank the Great Lady.

Dear readers, which historical places did you most enjoy visiting in 2025?

Leave a comment

PS. I’ve noticed that in every single one of these pictures I’m wearing either red or pink. Do you think I need to update my wardrobe? (The answer is always: ‘YES, Lucy. Of course you need a new dress.’)

Leave a comment

title: Tudor Christmas
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
The other day I found myself at a very upmarket carol service.

One of the lessons was read by Benedict Cumberbatch.

And another by, erm, me.

Afterwards the choristers all gathered round me, wanting my autograph. I was gratified, but confused about why they were asking me rather than the A-list Hollywood actor. Turned out the day before, in school, their history teacher had made them watch my programme A Merry Tudor Christmas.

My Life in the Past is having a half-price sale on annual subscriptions in December!

When the BBC first challenged me and a group of pals - including fabulous food historian Annie Gray - to re-stage a Tudor Christmas, my first thought was ‘this will be difficult’.

Anyone with even an inkling about the history of Christmas will know that the Victorians ‘invented’ the festival as we know it today. Christmas trees, crackers, cards: all appeared in the consumerist society Britain became after the Industrial Revolution.

But of course the Tudors still celebrated the birth of Christ. Our research revealed the ways they went about it were both reassuringly familiar, but also deeply strange.

For a start, the Tudors did the feasting and the fasting the other way round.

Advent, to them, wasn’t a time for eating chocolates out of calendars. Instead it was a period of abstinence - no meat, no dairy - in preparation for a religious festival which only began on 25 December.

Today society expects us to binge throughout December, and by January it’s time for healthy eating and the gym. But when we go back to work, the Tudors would still have been enjoying a holiday which lasted twelve days, culminating in the biggest party of all on 6th January.

But Tudor parties did foreshadow many familiar things.

For a start: what’s the smell of Christmas? It’s surely the scent of spices, from mulled wine, Christmas cake, scented candles. And for rich Tudors too, the food at their feasts would have included valuable spices – cloves, mace, pepper – imported from all round the globe.

There’s a big difference when it came to mince pies, though.

Tudor ones weren’t sugary little things. Theirs were massive, and made out of actual minced meat sweetened with dried fruit. A ‘mince’ pie gets its name from ‘mincing’ or chopping the ingredients into tiny bits. Over the last five hundred years, the pies themselves have shrunk and the meat has disappeared, although an echo of its presence just about lingers on in the form of the suet that makes still some of today’s mince pies non-vegetarian.

What about decorations? Long before the Tudors, the early Christians took over and repurposed the earlier pagan celebration of midwinter.

December, when the weather prevented people from doing farmwork or fighting wars, seemed as good time as any to pick to celebrate the birth of Jesus.

So the Tudors still maintained some ancient pre-Christian traditions such as bringing greenery into the house to give a sense of renewal.

Tudor housewives decorated their spinning wheels by anchoring the wheels to the frames with long stems of ivy to prevent any spinning from being done until the holiday was over.

And where do presents fit in? You might well keep a list of all people you send cards to. Henry VIII did something similar for gifts, although he gave his presents on New Year’s Day.

In the National Archives documents still survive recording what Henry gave to his family, nobles and courtiers – mainly costly objects like goblets made out of gold and silver – and what he received in return.

The list for 1532 is particularly interesting because there’s just an empty space for the gift Henry VIII gave to ‘the queen’.

That’s because Anne Boleyn was now on the scene, so poor Catherine of Aragon - who was, after all, still queen - didn’t get anything from her husband that year.

These gifts were exchanged in a very formal ceremony on 1st January, but Tudor courtiers let their hair down at their Twelfth Night masques and balls. Henry VIII loved to dress up in a disguise as a cover for misbehaviour.

Something of that survives in the form of the office Christmas party today, when the people who seem quiet and well-behaved all year appear in surprisingly sparkly dresses, drink too much, and photocopy their bums.

Subscribe now

At a Tudor court Christmas the normal social order got turned upside-down, and there was a kind of organised chaos overseen by a special employee called The Lord of Misrule. He was sometimes referred to as ‘Lord Christmas’, which makes historians think that he might have been the forerunner of our modern Father Christmas.

But the most significant Tudor thing that’s missing from many people’s Christmases today is probably religion. After all, the ‘twelve days’ represented saints’ feast days in the religious calendar.

And here perhaps the Tudors perhaps took a more spiritually healthy approach than we do. To them religion was so much a part of life that popping into church for a bit of mind-cleansing reflection and meditation was second nature.

In the midst of Tudor life, death was close at hand, whether through famine, disease or war, and I think this brought a sense of perspective and of gratitude that can be lacking from our modern orgy-of-shopping.

It’s been a joy and privilege to pop into your inbox this year, dear readers.

I send you all my very best (Tudor) Christmas wishes!

Leave a comment

title: Book Club: novels about historical murder
author: lucyworsley
raw_article:
This is the exciting week that LADY KILLERS is back on the radio and wherever you get your podcasts!

I’m having a half-price sale on annual subscriptions throughout December

We have a whole rainbow of different types of killer women for you in the new season: a high-society murderess in 1930s Mayfair, a female body snatcher, and an enslaved woman in Pennsylvania who drowned the children of the people who claimed to own her.

Each case seems more shocking, poignant and compelling than the last.

But the first episode was our special one-off murderous Book Club, which we had the great joy of convening back in the summer with lots of our lovely Lady Killers listeners, live, in a tent, in the pouring rain, at the Hay Festival.

We wanted to dissect novels featuring killer women, and who better to help us than one of my all-time top-favourite novelists, Sarah Waters?*

We were wild to have Sarah in our Book Club, partly because we worship her, but not least because her novel THE PAYING GUESTS has a loose relationship with the real events in the 1922 case of Edith Thompson.

Edith Thompson (of Ilford, East London) was convicted of helping to kill her husband Percy, on the very shaky grounds that she’d written some sexy letters to her lover.

Edith’s lover Freddy stabbed Percy one night on the streets as he walked home.

At the trial, there was a lot of public sympathy for the widowed Edith.

Until, that is, the prosecution produced some sexually explicit letters between Edith and her lover, full of what were seen as depraved fantasies.

Suddenly, it seemed, everyone could now see Edith as an accomplice in the crime. Surely a woman who could write those letters was capable of anything?

Other factors told against poor Edith too. She was one of the rather shocking ‘new women’ who were upsetting old buffers everywhere in the 1920s.

She had a job – a big job, which took her on business trips to Paris – but it was selling hats. Which had a frivolous lack of respectability. Note too the couple’s address: they were from East London, not the fancy West End.

Young, competent, fashionable, and unfaithful to her husband: taken together, the court thought that all this seemed to make her a killer.

There was recently an attempt to get Edith a pardon, on the grounds that so much of her ‘guilt’ rested on the letters she’d written to Freddy.

The commission reviewing the case even listened to the episode of Lady Killers which we devoted to Edith!

But sadly we didn’t manage to convince them, and Edith is still – at least in the eyes of the law – guilty.

Now, in Sarah Waters’ fabulous novel, THE PAYING GUESTS, set in the same period, there’s a similar central love triangle involving a married couple.

But this time of course, because this is a Sarah Waters novel, the wife’s lover is female. And the killing is … well, of course I’m not going to reveal the ending because I don’t want to spoil it for you.

Sarah told us she’d wanted to write a novel that hangs from a striking image she’d had in her mind – of two women carrying a corpse down the stairs.

Yes, how often do you see that in film or fiction? Not often enough!

Sarah explained how in the 1920s the relationship between the two women would have been invisible to society, which would have helped them to commit a crime. But it would also have left them terribly isolated.

There’s a half-price sale on annual subscriptions throughout December!

Re-reading THE PAYING GUESTS this summer, I was reminded how brilliantly it recreates the lives of middle and lower-middle class people (the distinctions are very important!) in outer London in the 1920s.

The slow burn of the first half is magnificent, building up such a detailed picture of the life of the heroine, a former Suffragette who’s by now basically a maid-of-all-work in a house that’s far too big for herself and her mother.

The cleaning of the tiled floor with vinegar! The lighting of the stove! The laborious peeling of the potatoes!

It’s all so grimily, grindingly realistic.

One of the things I love about Sarah Waters’ books in general is the close relationship they have to the literature being published at the time she’s writing about.

THE PAYING GUESTS is full of the language and customs and consumer goods and feelings of the empty, hollowed-out, post-War period, when people were beginning to realise that they weren’t just going to go back to life as it had been in Edwardian times. And if not that, then … what?

As people say at the end of Book Club meetings: my conclusion is that THE PAYING GUESTS is a historian’s historical novel, the perfect novel for lovers of Lady Killers, and a book I can’t recommend highly enough.

What have you read recently that you’ve enjoyed?

Leave a comment

*I’ll tell you a shaming story: I once got to have tea with Sarah Waters because with incomparable kindness, she bought me in a charity auction.

The ‘prize’ offered was to have afternoon tea with me, and when no one else at all was bidding. Sarah saved me from the humiliation of being unsold.

When she came to tea, I was naturally desperate to ask her where she gets her ideas from, and what kind of pen she uses, and all those idiotic things people are burning to ask their favourite novelists.

But I felt that wasn’t at all what she deserved for her kind deed, and I tried really hard to stop myself.
